description: This image presents two side-by-side maps that illustrate the effect of a majority filter resampling technique on building height data. The left panel shows a high-resolution orthophoto of an urban area, featuring a large, multi-section building with extensive green roofs, several skylights, and flat grey roof sections. Surrounding the building are roads, trees, pavements, and other urban structures. Blue lines precisely delineate the footprints of all visible buildings. The right panel displays a thematic raster map of the identical geographic area, depicting processed building height values. This map uses a colour gradient: dark red represents the lowest values (likely ground level), transitioning through pale green, orange, and light yellow to indicate progressively higher values. The main building structure, as seen in the orthophoto, corresponds to the light yellow and orange pixels in the thematic map, signifying greater height. Smaller adjacent structures appear in pale green, indicating lower heights than the main building. The blue building footprint outlines from the left panel are overlaid on the raster data in the right panel. This comparison visually explains how detailed, high-resolution imagery (implied 0.5-2 meter resolution from context) is transformed into a coarser, pixelated product (implied 10-meter spatial resolution) by applying a majority algorithm to derive height values for each resampled pixel.
